title: Redshift Query Queue Depth
spl:
index=aws sourcetype="aws:cloudwatch" namespace="AWS/Redshift" metric_name="WLMQueueDepth"
| timechart span=5m max(Maximum) as queue_depth by ClusterIdentifier, QueueName
| where queue_depth > 10
description: WLM queue length and max execution time show concurrency saturation. Growing queue depth precedes disk-based spills and timeouts.
value: WLM queue length and max execution time show concurrency saturation. Growing queue depth precedes disk-based spills and timeouts.
implementation: Map queue names to workload classes. Alert when queue_depth sustained above SLA. Tune WLM slots or concurrency scaling.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: CloudWatch, `STL_WLM_QUERY` export.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `WLMQueueDepth`, `WLMQueriesCompletedPerSecond`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Map queue names to workload classes. Alert when queue_depth sustained above SLA. Tune WLM slots or concurrency scaling.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=aws sourcetype="aws:cloudwatch" namespace="AWS/Redshift" metric_name="WLMQueueDepth"
| timechart span=5m max(Maximum) as queue_depth by ClusterIdentifier, QueueName
| where queue_depth > 10
```

Understanding this SPL

**Redshift Query Queue Depth** ‚Äî WLM queue length and max execution time show concurrency saturation. Growing queue depth precedes disk-based spills and timeouts.

Documented **Data sources**: `WLMQueueDepth`, `WLMQueriesCompletedPerSecond`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): CloudWatch, `STL_WLM_QUERY` export. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: aws; **sourcetype**: aws:cloudwatch. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=aws, sourcetype="aws:cloudwatch". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=5m** buckets with a separate series **by ClusterIdentifier, QueueName** ‚Äî ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where queue_depth > 10` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (queue depth), Table (cluster, queue, depth), Single value (max depth).
visualization: Line chart (queue depth), Table (cluster, queue, depth), Single value (max depth).
